unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! This unit is all about sports, the Olympics, and what it means to achieve something great. You will explore how athletes train, how the Olympic Games began in ancient Greece, and how the Paralympics have changed our understanding of what the human body can do. Along the way you will build powerful vocabulary, strengthen your grammar, and learn to write about sports with confidence and skill.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners analysing sport, Olympic achievement and inclusion

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A way of explaining something by comparing it to something else that is similar.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher used an analogy to explain how a computer works by comparing it to a brain.
practiceSentences: The teacher used an analogy to explain how a computer works by comparing it to a brain.
The word analogy belongs to our analysis and argument vocabulary.
I can explain analogy in my own words.
I can connect analogy to this unit's texts.
I can use analogy in a clear Grade 7 sentence.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - a - l - o - g - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'analogy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Sports, Olympics, and Achievement.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and understand non-fiction texts about sports and the Olympic Games.
Use important vocabulary such as analogy, bias, evidence, viewpoint, and more.
Recognise and form compound nouns (e.g. football, swimming pool, team-mate).
Identify abstract nouns that name ideas and feelings (e.g. courage, achievement, diversity).
Compare actions using comparative adverbs (e.g. more quickly, less carefully, better).
Write a sports profile and a match report using clear structure and lively detail.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Speak confidently about sports topics, present research, and discuss ethical questions.

readings.title: Sports, Olympics, and Achievement: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 6! This unit is all about sports, the Olympics, and what it means to achieve something great. You will explore how athletes train, how the Olympic Games began in ancient Greece, and how the Paralympics have changed our understanding of what the human body can do. Along the way you will build powerful vocabulary, strengthen your grammar, and learn to write about sports with confidence and skill.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand non-fiction texts about sports and the Olympic Games.
Use important vocabulary such as analogy, bias, evidence, viewpoint, and more.
Recognise and form compound nouns (e.g. football, swimming pool, team-mate).
Identify abstract nouns that name ideas and feelings (e.g. courage, achievement, diversity).
Compare actions using comparative adverbs (e.g. more quickly, less carefully, better).
Write a sports profile and a match report using clear structure and lively detail.
Speak confidently about sports topics, present research, and discuss ethical questions.
Think critically about inclusion, fairness, and achievement in sport.
Before you start reading, look through these important words. You will meet them again and again throughout the unit. A full vocabulary guide with definitions, examples, and practice activities is in your separate Vocabulary file.
analogy – explaining something by comparing it to something else
bias – an unfair preference that affects how you think
evidence – facts that help prove something is true
viewpoint – a way of thinking about something
diversity – having many different types of people or things
objective – a goal you want to achieve
resolution – a decision or promise to do something
structure – how something is built or organised
estimate – a careful guess about an amount
inevitable – certain to happen and impossible to avoid
Reading: The Spirit of the Olympics
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the words in bold — they are vocabulary words from this unit.
Every four years, athletes from around the world gather for the Olympic Games. The event is a celebration of sport, hard work, and human achievement. But the story of the Olympics stretches back thousands of years, to a time when ancient Greece hosted the very first competitions in a place called Olympia.
The ancient Games began around 776 BCE. At first, there was only one race — a short sprint called the stadion. Over time, the Greeks added wrestling, boxing, chariot racing, and the pentathlon. Athletes trained for months. Winning brought enormous honour, not just to the individual but to the athlete’s entire city-state. There was no silver or bronze in those days; only the winner received the olive wreath. The contrast between ancient and modern ceremonies is striking, yet the spirit of fair competition has remained the same.
The modern Olympics were revived in 1896 by Pierre de Coubertin, a French educator who believed sport could bring nations together. The first modern Games, held in Athens, featured just 241 athletes from 14 countries. Today, the Games welcome over 10,000 competitors from more than 200 nations — a powerful symbol of global diversity.
One of the most important developments in recent decades has been the growth of the Paralympic Games. The Paralympics celebrate athletes with physical disabilities, proving that courage and determination matter far more than physical limitations. Wheelchair basketball, sitting volleyball, and blade running have become some of the most exciting events to watch. An analogy that many coaches use is this: “Ability is like an iceberg — what you see on the surface is only a small part of the whole.”

readings.title: Sports, Olympics, and Achievement: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Yet the world of sport also faces serious challenges. Bias can influence decisions made by judges and officials. Doping — the use of banned substances to improve performance — threatens fair play. Some critics argue that hosting the Games costs too much money and that ordinary citizens rarely benefit. These are not easy problems, and people hold different viewpoints about how to solve them.
Despite these challenges, the Olympic movement continues to inspire. When a young athlete from a small village stands on the podium, the message is clear: with discipline, training, and a strong objective, extraordinary things are possible. The evidence is in every record broken, every personal best achieved, and every handshake between competitors who moments earlier were rivals.
Sport teaches us that success is not inevitable — it must be earned through effort and perseverance. Whether you play football in a dusty field or swim laps in an Olympic pool, the lessons are the same: set a goal, work hard, respect your opponents, and never stop improving. That is the true spirit of the Olympics, and it belongs to everyone.
Reading 2: A Day in the Life of a Young Athlete
Read this short text about a young runner and think about the discipline needed to succeed in sport.
Nadia wakes at five o'clock every morning, even on weekends. Before the sun rises over Hargeisa, she is already stretching on the dusty road outside her home. Running is more than a hobby for Nadia — it is her objective, her daily commitment, and the one thing that makes her feel truly alive.
Her training routine is strict. She runs eight kilometres before breakfast, rests during the hottest part of the day, and then does strength exercises in the afternoon. Her coach, a former national athlete, watches her progress carefully and keeps a training log so they can estimate how fast she is improving each month. "Numbers are evidence of hard work," he tells her. "They don't lie."
Nadia's dream is to compete at the East African Schools Championships. She knows the competition will be fierce — runners from Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, and Ethiopia train just as hard as she does. But she is not discouraged. "My mother always says that nothing worth having comes easily," Nadia explains. "When I feel tired, I remember that every step forward is a step closer to my goal."
Not everything is easy. Nadia sometimes struggles to balance training with her schoolwork. She misses social events because she has to sleep early. Some classmates do not understand why she works so hard. But her resolution is firm: she will represent her country one day. For Nadia, achievement is not about medals alone. It is about proving to herself that discipline and determination can overcome any obstacle.
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
What analogy do coaches use to describe ability? Explain what it means in your own words.
Name two challenges that the world of sport faces today, according to the passage.
These questions ask you to go beyond the text. There is no single correct answer — the important thing is to explain your thinking clearly.
The passage says some people believe hosting the Olympics costs too much. Do you agree or disagree? Give at least one reason.

readings.title: The Truth in the Margins, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“Everyone is talking about it,” said Ishmael, sliding his tray onto the lunch table. “Did you see the new story in the school magazine?”
Sophia looked up. “No. What’s it about?”
Ishmael lowered his voice. “It’s a piece about exam cheating — but it blames the Form 3 students. People are angry. They say it’s full of bias.”
Sophia blinked. She was the editor of The Crescent, the school’s monthly publication. As far as she knew, no article like that had been approved. She stood up and walked straight to the notice board.
There it was: a stapled page titled “The Downfall of Discipline.”
She read quickly. The article claimed that cheating had become inevitable, and it made sharp comparisons between current students and “the golden classes of the past.” There was a dramatic climax accusing one entire stream of “undermining the school’s values.”
“No name. No byline,” she murmured. “But it’s written like an opinion piece. And it’s not neutral.”
The problem wasn’t just the anger. It was the structure. The article mixed opinion and fact with no clear introduction or logical organization. Worse, it didn’t use proper evidence. It quoted unnamed sources and offered no balance in viewpoint.
By afternoon, students from Form 3 were yelling in the corridor. One group accused the editorial team of targeting them. Another group defended the piece, saying it was an “honest reflection.”
That evening, Sophia called a meeting in the journalism room. Everyone on the team came — except Faith, the assistant editor.
“I didn’t publish that,” Sophia said. “It wasn’t part of the approved structure.”
“Then how did it get printed?” Ishmael asked.
Sophia paused. “We only printed five copies. Someone added a sixth.”
Ishmael frowned. “So someone illustrated their opinion with false facts.”
“It’s more serious than that,” Sophia said. “It could damage our school’s unity. The implication is that a whole group is dishonest.”
The next morning, the head teacher summoned her. “This has become a judicial matter within the school. The article sparked complaints. You need to respond.”
Sophia nodded. “We’re preparing a public resolution. And I want to publish a follow-up article that explains the context.”
The head teacher tapped his pen. “Just be careful. More damage could happen if the issue isn’t handled more objectively.”
Back at the newsroom, the team argued over what to do next. One writer said, “Let’s write an angry reply. Expose whoever did it.”
Ishmael shook his head. “That’s just argumentative for no reason. We need to think.”
Sophia took a deep breath. “We should focus on explaining the process. Use facts. Include quotations. And explore multiple viewpoints.”
“We’ll write a new piece,” she said, “called The Truth in the Margins. It’ll have a clear introduction, supported by real evidence, and offer alternative voices. It’ll ask questions instead of blaming.”
They began the next day. Sophia gathered real accounts from students, teachers, and prefects. Ishmael wrote an excerpt about the history of cheating incidents. They included a summary of school rules and policies and made sure each paragraph had proper organization.
“I’ll also add a section about censorship,” Ishmael said. “So readers understand why unofficial publications matter.”
Faith arrived at the newsroom midweek, red-faced and silent. After a long pause, she whispered, “I added the article. I thought it would create discussion.”

readings.title: The Truth in the Margins, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sophia stared at her. “But without approval?”
“I’m sorry,” Faith said. “I just wanted to raise the issue. I didn’t expect it to spread.”
Sophia could have been angry. But she saw something in Faith’s face — fear and regret.
“We can’t undo it,” she said. “But we can illustrate the bigger issue. Why did you feel unheard?”
Faith’s eyes welled. “I felt like no one was acknowledging the real pressure we’re under. We act like everything’s fine, but it’s not.”
The team sat quietly. Then Ishmael said, “Maybe that’s the real story.”
They added a new section: Behind the Panic: Student Voices. It showed anonymous student reflections on stress, comparing the pressure now to earlier years. They used an analogy: “Stress is like a rope. The more you pull, the more likely it snaps.”
They also estimated that over 80% of senior students worried about exams daily.
The piece included proper structure, used neutral language, and had diverse perspectives. One line read: “Some believe discipline has dwindled; others say expectations have changed. The truth may lie in the contrast.”
On Friday, they published the corrected edition.
The school read it during homeroom. It did not cause a riot. It caused reflection.
One teacher said, “It’s far more concisely written than the first.”
Another said, “It’s balanced. I see no bias.”
Faith was not punished. But she did write a short note in the next issue, acknowledging the importance of proper publishing process.
Over the next weeks, students submitted more writing — essays about resilience, opinion pieces about uniform rules, even poems about exam fear.
Ishmael grinned. “Our next edition is going to be big.”
Sophia smiled. “And far less chaotic.”
At the end of the term, The Crescent was nominated for a regional schools writing award.
As they gathered for the celebration, Sophia reflected: organization, truth, and diversity don’t just make good stories — they build strong communities.
In a world where censorship, bias, and disconnection can spread quickly, storytelling had become their tool for clarity.
And in the margin of that moment, truth had finally found its voice.

grammar.title: Compound Nouns
grammar.explanation:
What is a compound noun?
A compound noun is made by combining two or more words to create a new noun with a specific meaning. Understanding compound nouns helps you express yourself precisely and makes your writing richer.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What is a compound noun?
A compound noun is made by combining two or more words to create a new noun with a specific meaning. Understanding compound nouns helps you express yourself precisely and makes your writing richer.
How to form compound nouns
Step 1: Identify two words that can work together to create a new idea.
Step 2: Combine them in one of three ways:
Closed form — no space: football, goalkeeper, notebook, teamwork
Hyphenated form — joined with a hyphen: runner-up, team-mate, half-time, mother-in-law
Open form — two separate words: swimming pool, world record, ice cream, starting line
Step 3: Make sure you understand the new meaning. In football, the word foot describes the kind of ball sport. In world record, the word world describes the scale of the record.
Step 4: Use it in a sentence. Example: "I created a summary table for my project."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Spacing errors: Writing "goal keeper" instead of goalkeeper, or "tooth brush" instead of toothbrush. Always check whether the compound is closed, hyphenated, or open.
Missing hyphens: Writing "runner up" instead of runner-up or "mother in law" instead of mother-in-law.
Meaning confusion: Some compound nouns have meanings that are not obvious from the individual words. For example, football means different sports in different countries. Always check the meaning in context.
Practice 1
Complete each sentence with the correct compound noun formed from the words in brackets.
I need to buy a new __________ (tooth + brush) because mine is old.
Can you check the __________ (score + board) to see who is winning?
The __________ (goal + keeper) made an incredible save in the last minute.
The __________ (swimming + pool) at our school is twenty-five metres long.
The __________ (half + time) break gave the team a chance to rest.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply compound nouns in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Abstract Nouns
grammar.explanation:
What is an abstract noun?
Abstract nouns are words that name things you cannot see, touch, or measure with your hands. They represent ideas, feelings, qualities, or states. Understanding abstract nouns helps you express thoughts and emotions clearly and makes your writing more powerful.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What is an abstract noun?
Abstract nouns are words that name things you cannot see, touch, or measure with your hands. They represent ideas, feelings, qualities, or states. Understanding abstract nouns helps you express thoughts and emotions clearly and makes your writing more powerful.
How to form abstract nouns
Most abstract nouns come from adjectives or verbs. Here are the most common patterns:
Adjective + -ness: happy → happiness, kind → kindness, conscious → consciousness
Adjective + -ity: diverse → diversity, inevitable → inevitability
Adjective + -ry / -ery: brave → bravery
Verb + -tion / -sion: organise → organisation, resolve → resolution, introduce → introduction
Verb + -ment: achieve → achievement, argue → argument
Noun + -ship: friend → friendship, sportsman → sportsmanship
Noun + -hood: child → childhood, neighbour → neighbourhood
Real sentence examples
The evidence in the case was very convincing.
The theme of the story is about friendship.
We learned about the resolution of the conflict in the play.
Good organisation is key to writing a great essay.
Her bravery during the competition inspired the whole team.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using the adjective instead of the noun: Saying "the brave of the athlete" instead of "the bravery of the athlete." Always check that the form matches the role.
Forgetting to change form: Saying "the organise was good" instead of "the organisation was good."
Confusing abstract and concrete: Remember, abstract nouns are things you cannot physically touch. You can hold a medal (concrete), but you cannot hold courage (abstract).
Practice 2
Complete each sentence with the correct abstract noun form of the word in brackets.
The teacher praised the student’s __________ (organise).
We discussed the __________ (happy) of the main character.
His __________ (brave) during the rescue was admirable.
__________ (diverse) is what makes the Olympic Games so special.
The team’s __________ (determine) carried them to victory.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply abstract nouns in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Pronouns — Everyone, Someone, Anyone
grammar.explanation:
What are indefinite pronouns?
Pronouns like everyone, someone, and anyone are called indefinite pronouns. They refer to people in a general way, without naming anyone specifically. Using them well makes your English sound natural and fluent.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What are indefinite pronouns?
Pronouns like everyone, someone, and anyone are called indefinite pronouns. They refer to people in a general way, without naming anyone specifically. Using them well makes your English sound natural and fluent.
How to use them
Use everyone when you mean all people: "Everyone is excited about the match."
Use someone when you mean one person but you do not know who: "Someone left their water bottle on the pitch."
Use anyone when you mean any person at all: "Anyone can join the training session."
Important rule: These pronouns are always singular, even though they refer to many people. Use singular verbs with them:
Correct: "Everyone is happy."   Wrong: "Everyone are happy."
Correct: "Someone has a question."   Wrong: "Someone have a question."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using a plural verb: "Everyone are ready" should be "Everyone is ready."
Pronoun agreement: In informal English, we often say "Everyone brought their kit." In formal writing, some guides prefer "his or her", but "their" is now widely accepted.
Practice 3
Choose the correct pronoun (everyone, someone, anyone) and verb form to complete each sentence.
__________ in the stadium __________ (was/were) cheering for the home team.
__________ __________ (has/have) forgotten their sports kit in the changing room.
__________ who wants to play in the tournament __________ (need/needs) to sign up today.
__________ on the team __________ (deserve/deserves) a medal for their effort.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply pronouns — everyone, someone, anyone in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Comparative Adverbs
grammar.explanation:
What are comparative adverbs?
Comparative adverbs help us compare how actions are done. If you want to say that one person runs faster than another, or that a team played more skilfully in the second half, you use a comparative adverb. They make your descriptions of sport vivid and precise.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What are comparative adverbs?
Comparative adverbs help us compare how actions are done. If you want to say that one person runs faster than another, or that a team played more skilfully in the second half, you use a comparative adverb. They make your descriptions of sport vivid and precise.
How to form comparative adverbs
Step 1: Identify the adverb. Adverbs often end in "-ly" but not always. Examples: quickly, clearly, fast, hard, well.
Step 2: Decide how to form the comparative:
Short adverbs (usually one syllable): add -er. fast → faster, hard → harder, early → earlier.
Longer adverbs (two or more syllables, often ending in -ly): use more or less. quickly → more quickly, carefully → less carefully, clearly → more clearly.
Irregular adverbs: well → better, badly → worse, far → further (or farther).
Step 3: Always use than when comparing two actions: "She runs faster than her opponent."
Real sentence examples
"My sister studies more diligently than I do."
"He speaks less clearly when he is nervous."
"This book explains the topic more thoroughly than the last one."
"She plays football better than anyone else on the team."
"The second runner finished more quickly than expected."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using more with short adverbs: "She runs more fast" should be "She runs faster."
Forgetting than: "She runs more quickly" is incomplete. Add: "She runs more quickly than her friend."
Double comparatives: "She runs more faster" is wrong. Use either "more quickly" or "faster", never both together.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence using the correct comparative form of the adverb in brackets.
My brother plays video games __________ (skilfully) than I do.
She explained the tactics __________ (thoroughly) than the assistant coach.
He runs __________ (fast) than anyone else in the squad.
The team defended __________ (well) in the second half than in the first.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply comparative adverbs in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Expressing the Future
grammar.explanation:
What does it mean?
When we talk about the future in English, we express things that will happen later. This is essential for discussing plans, making predictions, and talking about hopes and goals — all important in the world of sport.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What does it mean?
When we talk about the future in English, we express things that will happen later. This is essential for discussing plans, making predictions, and talking about hopes and goals — all important in the world of sport.
Three ways to express the future
1. Will + base verb — for predictions, promises, and decisions made at the moment of speaking:
"I will train harder this season."
"The match will start at three o’clock."
2. Be going to + base verb — for plans already made and predictions based on evidence:
"She is going to compete in the national championships."
"Look at those clouds — it is going to rain during the match."
3. Present continuous for future arrangements — for fixed plans in the near future:
"We are playing against Hillside School tomorrow."
"The coach is arriving at noon."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Using will for definite plans: "I will go to practice on Friday" should be "I am going to go to practice on Friday" if you have already decided.
Confusing present continuous with simple present: "I see my coach tomorrow" should be "I am seeing my coach tomorrow."
Practice 5
Complete each sentence using will, am/is/are going to, or the present continuous form of the verb in brackets.
I __________ (write) my match report this weekend.
She __________ (present) her research on the Olympics next Monday.
We __________ (discuss) the results in our next lesson.
They __________ (finish) their training before the tournament begins.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply expressing the future in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Grade 7 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read a paragraph aloud. Check sentence completeness, agreement, tense, joining words and punctuation. Revise one feature at a time.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.
grammar.practice: Edit a four-paragraph response using the unit language focus. Mark eight changes and explain how four changes improve clarity and cohesion.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Sports Debate
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
A good debate helps you practise giving opinions with evidence. Work with your AI tutor or a partner. Choose one of these debate topics:
"The Olympic Games should be held in the same city every time."
"Paralympic athletes deserve the same prize money as Olympic athletes."
"Professional athletes are paid too much money."
"Traditional games are more valuable than modern sports."
For your chosen topic:
Decide whether you agree or disagree.
Prepare three reasons to support your position. Use words like evidence, viewpoint, and alternative.
Present your argument for one minute, speaking clearly and confidently.
Listen to the opposing view and respond respectfully.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise sports debate and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Olympic Research Presentation
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one of the following topics and prepare a short presentation of about two minutes:
The history of the Olympic Games: from ancient Greece to today.
A sport that is popular in your community: its rules, equipment, and why people enjoy it.
A Paralympic sport: what it is, who competes, and why it matters.
A famous Muslim athlete: their background, achievements, and what you admire about them.
Your presentation should have a clear structure: an introduction, a middle section with facts and examples, and a conclusion. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit. Practise with your AI tutor before presenting to your teacher.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise olympic research presentation and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Sports Interview Role-Play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise an interview with your AI tutor or a partner. One person plays a famous athlete, and the other plays a sports journalist.
The journalist should ask at least five questions. Here are some ideas:
"How did you first become interested in your sport?"
"What was the most difficult moment in your career, and how did you overcome it?"
"What advice would you give to young athletes who want to follow in your footsteps?"
"How do you stay motivated when training becomes hard?"
"What is your objective for the next competition?"
The athlete should answer in full sentences, using vocabulary from this unit where possible. After the interview, swap roles so both people get to practise.
Listening Practice: Sports Commentary
Ask your AI tutor to read you a short sports commentary. Listen carefully, then answer these questions without looking at the text:
Which sport is being described?
What key event happened during the match?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise sports interview role-play and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Five-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a five-minute talk using an opening, three connected claims with evidence, a considered viewpoint, a response to an alternative view, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise five-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 2: Sports Profile
writing.promptAndInstructions:
A sports profile is a short piece of writing that introduces a sportsperson to the reader. It gives key facts, describes achievements, and includes a personal touch that makes the person come alive on the page.
Choose a sportsperson you admire — this can be a famous international athlete, a local player, or even someone from your school or community. Write a sports profile of 200 to 250 words.
Your profile should include:
Introduction: Full name, sport, and country or city of origin.
Background: How they started, what inspired them, any challenges they faced.
Achievements: Key results, records, awards. Use at least one compound noun (e.g. world record, team-mate, championship title).
Qualities: What makes them special? Use at least one abstract noun (e.g. determination, courage, resilience).
Your opinion: Why do you admire this person? End with a strong closing sentence.
Model opening (for guidance):
Mo Ishmael is a long-distance runner from Somalia who represented Great Britain at the Olympic Games. Born in Mogadishu, he moved to London as a young boy and discovered his love for running at school. His determination and hard work earned him four Olympic gold medals, making him one of the greatest distance runners of all time.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 7 response uses cohesive paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence, source awareness and a purposeful conclusion about sports profile.
writing.sentenceStarter: I would like to argue or explain...
writing.successCriteria: I addressed the prompt and audience; organised ideas into cohesive paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported claims with relevant evidence; considered viewpoint and source reliability; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan claims, topic sentences and supporting evidence, then use the word bank and source models.
writing.extension: Synthesize details from more than one text, acknowledge an alternative viewpoint, vary sentence structures and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: Match Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
A match report describes what happened during a sporting event. It is a type of non-fiction writing that uses clear, factual language with some descriptive detail to bring the event to life.
Think of a match or sporting event you have watched or played in. Write a match report of 200 to 250 words.
Your report should include:
Headline: A short, attention-grabbing title (e.g. "Eagles Soar to Victory in Thrilling Final").
Opening paragraph: Who played? When and where? What was the final result?
Key moments: Describe two or three important moments from the match. Use at least one comparative adverb (e.g. "The striker moved more quickly than the defenders").
Outcome and reaction: How did the match end? How did the players or crowd react?
Closing sentence: A comment on what the result means for the team or the season.
Model opening (for guidance):
On Saturday afternoon, Al-Ibrahim FC defeated Hilaal United 3-1 in a thrilling cup final at the National Stadium. The home side started more aggressively than their opponents, and midfielder Ishmael Ishmael opened the scoring in the fifteenth minute with a powerful long-range shot.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 7 response uses cohesive paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence, source awareness and a purposeful conclusion about match report.

writing.title: Writing 4: Comparison Paragraph
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a paragraph of 100 to 150 words comparing the ancient Olympic Games with the modern Olympic Games. Use the information from the reading passage and your own research to make at least three clear points of comparison.
Your paragraph should:
Begin with a clear topic sentence that introduces the comparison.
Use at least two comparative adverbs (e.g. more widely, less frequently, better).
Include at least one compound noun (e.g. city-state, opening ceremony, world record).
End with a sentence explaining which version of the Games you find more interesting and why.
Useful sentence starters for comparisons:
"The ancient Games were organised more simply than the modern Games because..."
"Today, athletes compete more frequently than in ancient times, with events held..."
"While the ancient Olympics featured fewer events, the modern Games include..."
"The opening ceremony is planned more elaborately today than it was in ancient Greece."
Peer Review Checklist

quizzes.question: What does 'analogy' mean?
quizzes.options: A goal or aim that you want to achieve. | A way of explaining something by comparing it to something else that is similar. | An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act. | Facts or information that help prove something is true.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way of explaining something by comparing it to something else that is similar.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher used an analogy to explain how a computer works by comparing it to a brain.

quizzes.question: What does 'argumentative' mean?
quizzes.options: A goal or aim that you want to achieve. | A way of thinking about something; a perspective. | Facts or information that help prove something is true. | Someone who likes to argue or disagree with others.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Someone who likes to argue or disagree with others.
quizzes.explanation: My friend can be very argumentative when we talk about our favourite movies.

quizzes.question: What does 'bias' mean?
quizzes.options: A goal or aim that you want to achieve. | A way of thinking about something; a perspective. | An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act. | The most exciting or important part of a story or event.
quizzes.correctAnswer: An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher tried to be fair, but some students felt she had a bias towards the football players.

quizzes.question: What does 'evidence' mean?
quizzes.options: A way of thinking about something; a perspective. | Facts or information that help prove something is true. | The most exciting or important part of a story or event. | The situation or background that helps you understand something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Facts or information that help prove something is true.
quizzes.explanation: The detective found evidence that showed who took the missing money.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Sports, Olympics, and Achievement: source text 1': Use important vocabulary such as _____, bias, evidence, viewpoint, and more.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Use important vocabulary such as analogy, bias, evidence, viewpoint, and more.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Truth in the Margins, part 1': “That’s just _____ for no reason.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “That’s just argumentative for no reason.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Sports, Olympics, and Achievement: source text 2': _____ can influence decisions made by judges and officials.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Bias can influence decisions made by judges and officials.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Sports, Olympics, and Achievement: source text 2': The _____ is in every record broken, every personal best achieved, and every handshake between competitors who moments earlier were rivals.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The evidence is in every record broken, every personal best achieved, and every handshake between competitors who moments earlier were rivals.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Sports, Olympics, and Achievement: source text 2': When a young athlete from a small village stands on the podium, the message is clear: with discipline, training, and a strong _____, extraordinary things are possible.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: When a young athlete from a small village stands on the podium, the message is clear: with discipline, training, and a strong objective, extraordinary things are possible.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Truth in the Margins, part 1': It quoted unnamed sources and offered no balance in _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: It quoted unnamed sources and offered no balance in viewpoint.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model disciplinary language aloud, teach annotation and source checking explicitly, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on evidence, cohesion, viewpoint and vocabulary precision while increasing learner independence.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A way of explaining something by comparing it to something else that is similar. The teacher used an analogy to explain how a computer works by comparing it to a brain.
Someone who likes to argue or disagree with others. My friend can be very argumentative when we talk about our favourite movies.
An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act. The teacher tried to be fair, but some students felt she had a bias towards the football players.
Facts or information that help prove something is true. The detective found evidence that showed who took the missing money.
A goal or aim that you want to achieve. My objective for this year is to improve my writing skills.
A way of thinking about something; a perspective. Everyone has a different viewpoint on what makes a good athlete.
The most exciting or important part of a story or event. The climax of the match was when the striker scored in the last minute.
The situation or background that helps you understand something. To understand the article, we need to look at the context in which it was written.
A short part taken from a longer piece of writing. The teacher read an excerpt from the book to the class.
The beginning part of something, where you explain what it is about. My essay starts with an introduction that states my main argument.
